So we have several algorithms that go into implementing neural nets.
The first one is graph construction.
The second one is forward propagation.
And graph construction is basically constructing the graph,
declaring all the variables and stuff like this.
The second one is forward propagation.
And the way you do this is in topological order.
You compute the value of a node given its inputs.
And so basically you start out with all of the nodes
that you give is input.
And then you find any node in the graph
where all of its tail nodes or all of its children
have been calculated.
So in this case, that would be these two nodes.
And then in arbitrary order or even in parallel,
you calculate the value of all of the satisfied nodes
until you hit the end.
And then the remaining algorithms are back propagation
and parameter update.
I already talked about parameter update
using stochastic gradient descent.
But for back propagation, we then process examples
in reverse topological order and calculate derivatives
and parameters with respect to the final value.
And so we start out with the very final value.
Usually this is your loss function.
And then you just step backwards in topological order
to calculate the derivatives and all of these.
